data.title: Genesco shines at ICR as its 2026 rally continues
data.description:
Genesco (GCO) has emerged as one of the stars of the ICR Conference. The footwear apparel company announced that comparable sales, including both stores and direct sales, were up 9% for the quarter-to-date period that ended on December 27.

Jefferies analyst Corey Tarlowe highlighted that the Journeys business saw comparable sales up at a double-digit, benefiting from newness and the 4.0 remodel. The Schuh business posted better results despite UK promotions, while the Johnston & Murphy business saw positive comparable sales with help from the Peyton Manning partnership. 

Genesco's (GCO) partnership with Peyton Manning is centered on making him the lead brand ambassador and marketing face for its Johnston & Murphy business, using his persona to drive traffic, awareness, and customer acquisition for the brand. The relationship launched with Johnston & Murphy’s Fall 2025 campaign and has already been highlighted by Genesco management as a contributor to improved comps and store traffic.​Shares of Genesco (GCO) were up 8.2% in Tuesday afternoon trading and are up more than 40% for the early part of 2026.

data.title: Intel, AMD lead in daily gains as most semiconductor stocks show mixed action
data.description:
Intel (INTC) shares popped up 8% by early afternoon trading on Tuesday as much of the semiconductor sector was demonstrating mixed action.

AMD (AMD) was also up 6.3% after KeyBanc reported that both companies were nearly sold out of CPUs for 2026 due to data center and artificial intelligence demand. 

Meanwhile, Nvidia (NVDA) had inched up 0.5% following a report that China had approved tech companies purchasing H200 GPUs in some cases.

However, Arm (ARM) had declined 3.6% as analysts pointed out it was facing risks due to near-term smartphone unit headwinds (memory cost), increasing reliance on SoftBank (SFTBY)(SFTBF) in licensing, and still small data center and AI exposure (only about 10% of royalties).

Memory stocks were mixed today following a recent and rampant run-up in prices due to clamoring demand. Micron Technology (MU) was down 2%, and Sandisk (SNDK) had inched down 0.5%, while Seagate Technology (STX) and Western Digital (WDC) were both up 2%.   

Credo Technology (CRDO) continued its recent run as shares popped up 4.5%. Its share value has increased nearly 17% over the past five trading sessions. Broadcom (AVGO) had ticked up 1.5%, while Marvell (MRVL) was up 0.6%. Qualcomm (QCOM) had slipped 1.5%.

Major semiconductor equipment companies were showing mixed results as well. Lam Research (LRCX) was down 1%, ASML Holding (ASML) was relatively flat, and KLA (KLAC) had edged up 1.4%.

data.title: U.S. stocks slip as retail inflation comes in slightly cooler, earnings fail to lift sentiment
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Tuesday, with the Nasdaq Composite ((COMP:IND)) showing some strength, as investors assessed cooler-than-expected core CPI inflation and some important earnings reports.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.2% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was flat, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.6%.

The December Consumer Price Index came in at +0.3% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.7% YoY vs. +2.6% consensus and +2.7% prior.

Core CPI (excludes food and energy) came in at +0.2% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.6% YoY vs. +2.7% consensus and +2.6% prior.

“Tuesday's CPI was in-line with expectations and that will likely keep the Federal Reserve on pause when it comes to interest rates for the foreseeable future… Recent positive employment data, elevated inflation, sticky price levels and political noise will keep the Fed at bay through at least the Spring,” said Skyler Weinand, CIO at Regan Capital.

“CPI is still under a cloud from the missing October number. Inflation is still relatively high, but trending downward. Consumers are still grappling with high overall price levels and sticker shock from the post-Covid jarring in housing, healthcare, education and everyday purchase prices,” Weinand added.

Also, new home sales slipped 0.1% MoM to 737K in October, vs. 716K expected, from 738K in the prior month, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Among companies reporting on Tuesday, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) -3.5% posted better-than-expected earnings, while Delta Air Lines (DAL) -2.8% had year-over-year revenue growth. 

Big companies reporting this week include Bank of America (BAC), Goldman Sachs (GS), and Citigroup (C).

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was flat at 4.18%, while the U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was also unchanged at 3.54%. Investors are awaiting a 30-Year bond auction later this afternoon.

data.title: Delivery giants face share loss, Amazon threat, leading BNP Paribas to downgrade FedEx and UPS
data.description:
• BNP Paribas downgraded the two giants in the delivery business, taking a dim view of the growth potential for FedEx (FDX) and UPS (UPS), undermined by the emergence of Amazon (AMZN) as a significant competitor, and “volume rationalization” that will limit earnings growth for UPS (UPS).
  • While the firm prefers FedEx (FDX) over UPS (UPS), FedEx (FDX) is not immune to the broader competitive pressures in U.S. domestic shipping. BNP Paribas anticipates a solid FY26 for FDX, but amid the rise of Amazon (AMZN) with its next-day offering and zip code coverage continually improving, the competitive pressure on FDX is expected to continue.
  • For UPS, lost market share, namely its Amazon (AMZN) volume, makes BNP Paribas uncertain as to the company’s ability to “grow earnings despite a potential yield benefit from any mix change.” The firm’s FY26 EBIT estimate now sits 9% below consensus. And with a new price target of $85 that suggests 20% downside, the firm downgrades UPS (UPS) to Underperform from Neutral.
  • Shares of both UPS (UPS) and FedEx (FDX) are both underwater on Tuesday.

data.title: HHS secretary RFK Jr. adds two new members to CDC vaccine panel
data.description:
Health and Human Services ((HHS)) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr., on Tuesday named two experts in obstetrics and gynecology as the newest members of the U.S. CDC’s Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices ((ACIP)).

The appointees are Adam Urato, a specialist in maternal-fetal medicine, and Kimberly Biss, a fellow of the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists whose publications include research on COVID-19 vaccine safety during pregnancy.

“Dr. Urato and Dr. Biss bring the scientific credentials, clinical experience, and integrity this committee requires,” RFK Jr. said in a statement.

The ACIP panel, which can include up to 19 members, meets three times a year to issue non-binding recommendations to the CDC regarding U.S. immunization policy. Their views play a key role in determining which vaccines are covered by health insurers at zero cost to the American public.

Secretary Kennedy fired all 17 ACIP members in June 2025, citing conflicts of interest and other concerns. He later appointed eight new members, and subsequently, one member withdrew from the committee just ahead of its June 25–26 meeting.

Just ahead of the ACIP’s Sept. 18–19 meeting, Kennedy named five new members to the panel, expanding it to 12.

Leading vaccine makers: Pfizer (PFE), AstraZeneca (AZN), Merck (MRK), GSK (GSK), Sanofi (SNY) (SNYNF), Moderna (MRNA), BioNTech (BNTX), and Novavax (NVAX)

data.title: Reviving Venezuela’s oil industry will take years, cost billions of dollars, TotalEnergies CEO says
data.description:
TotalEnergies (TTE) is in no rush to return to Venezuela, where it would take years to increase oil output to 3M bbl/day from less than 1M bbl/day currently and cost $100B, CEO Patrick Pouyanne said Tuesday.

"I know that people want ‍to rush there but ​it ‍will require a clear framework to be able to invest there ​and it will take time," Pouyanne said at Abu Dhabi Sustainability Week. "Maybe you could easily add 100K-200K bbl/day, but if you think about 1M bbl/day of oil, it will require $100B."

TotalEnergies (TTE), which began operating in Venezuela in the 1990s, held a 30% stake in a joint venture producing extra-heavy crude from the country's Orinoco Belt but left in 2022 and took a $1.38B write-down; unlike some U.S. peers, it is not owed money in the country.

"We were obliged to leave because the safety conditions were not there," Pouyanne said. "I'm not convinced [Venezuela] will have a direct impact on the market in '26... we are always evaluating, but it's not high on my agenda."

President Trump has urged U.S. energy companies to rebuild Venezuela's oil industry but threatened to keep Exxon Mobil out of the country after its CEO also voiced caution on Venezuela, calling the country "uninvestable."

data.title: Why it’s a ‘really good environment’ for banks despite policy risks – former Barclays CEO
data.description:
Bob Diamond, Atlas Merchant Capital CEO and former head of Barclays, sees a “terrific environment” ahead for the banking sector (KBE), (KBWB), (KRE), (XLF) in 2026, citing favorable regulatory conditions, a positive yield curve, and expectations for increased deal-making activity.

Diamond described U.S. banks (KBE) as a “very confident group” that is “performing extremely well.”

In an interview with CNBC, Diamond pointed to the current administration’s approach to bank regulation as a key driver of optimism.

“This administration has been very balanced, very positive on bank capital, maybe coming down a little, being more clear in terms of the requirements,” he said, adding that rate cuts and deal-making conditions should further boost the sector.

Diamond highlighted recent strong performances from specific institutions, noting that Bank of New York (BK) has “really improved over the last couple of reporting periods” and that Wells Fargo’s (WFC) investment banking business has shown notable gains.

He expects the administration’s willingness to be “more proactive in terms of approving deals generally” will create additional opportunities.

Despite the bullish outlook, Diamond acknowledged that successful banks tend to exercise caution during good times.

“The better the environment and the better your earnings, the more cautious you are about the outlook and the more willing you are to say, these are all the things to worry about,” he explained.

Policy risks, including potential interest rate caps on credit cards, remain a concern.

Diamond also emphasized overlooked opportunities in regional and community banking, noting that “there’s 4,500 banks in this country” beyond the well-known top institutions.

“The opportunity for them to merge, to acquire, to consolidate, to take costs out, to take advantage of AI, to take advantage of technology, we’re very focused on that,” he said.

While consolidation has not accelerated as quickly as expected since the SVB crisis, Diamond sees signs of a pickup. He identified banks in the $5B-50B range as particularly attractive, especially for “in state opportunities where you get to combine kind of the client front end, but take out the technology cost.”

data.title: Crypto data firm CoinGecko explores potential $500M sale - report
data.description:
Crypto market data and analytics platform CoinGecko has hired investment bank Moelis (MC) to advise on a potential sale, according to a media report on Tuesday.

The company is seeking a ~$500M valuation, CoinDesk reported, citing two people with knowledge. 

Still, a third source told CoinDesk that it's too early to set a valuation because the process only got underway late last year. 

CoinGecko didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Crypto M&A has been on a tear since pro-crypto President Donald Trump took office last year. And the momentum carried into 2026, with two crypto-focused entities filing for initial public offerings this week alone.

data.title: China approves tech companies buying Nvidia H200 GPUs in some cases: report
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) shares rose nearly 2% in midday trading after The Information reported that China had approved tech companies purchasing H200 GPUs in some cases. 

The Chinese government told some local companies that H200 AI accelerators could be purchased under special circumstances, citing examples as university research or development labs, the news outlet added, citing two people with direct knowledge of the situation. The missive from Chinese government officials was also vague, as they told the companies to buy the products if it was “necessary,” but did not specify what necessary meant, the news outlet added.

Additional meetings between the Chinese government and tech companies are expected on the matter, the sources said. 

Nvidia did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

China reportedly told some of its tech companies earlier this month to halt orders of Nvidia's H200 GPUs before the government made a decision on whether to allow the chips to be sold in the country.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said earlier this month week he did not believe the Chinese government will make a formal declaration that it has allowed Chinese companies to import the chipmaker's H200 GPUs. Instead, the proof would come through purchase orders.

Last year, President Donald Trump approved the sale of some advanced AI chips, including Nvidia's H200 GPUs, to China. The H200 is the predecessor to its current flagship Blackwell chips and the upcoming Vera Rubin.

Nvidia's CFO Colette Kress told analysts during a meeting at CES 2026 in Las Vegas last week that license applications for the H200 had been filed, and the government was deciding what it wants to do with them. Huang had described the demand for the chips as strong.

data.title: Morgan Stanley's PE unit takes majority stake in engineering firm Olsson
data.description:
Investment funds managed by Morgan Stanley's (MS) private equity team have made a majority investment in employee-owned engineering and design firm Olsson, Inc., according to a Tuesday statement.

Founded in 1956, Nebraska-based Olsson offers a suite of infrastructure engineering design and consulting solutions to both public and private clients across various end markets, including technology, transportation and water infrastructure, power, industrial, federal, among others.

“We are thrilled to be Olsson’s first institutional partner and believe that the Company is uniquely positioned to scale into a market leader in its targeted geographies and markets," said Eric Kanter, head of Industrial Services at Morgan Stanley Capital Partners ("MSCP"), the middle-market-focused PE unit at Morgan Stanley Investment Management.

MSCP's investment in Olsson marks its fourth investment in infrastructure services, following stakes in Resource Innovations, Apex Companies and Alliance Technical Group since 2021.
